title: What does your AI trading agent actually do during low-volatility / choppy markets? Sharing what mine does (and doesn't do)
author: ok-hacker
selftext:
This is something I've been thinking about a lot lately.



I've been running an autonomous trading agent on Solana for the past several months — it trades 24/7, sets its own entries and exits, and writes out its reasoning before every trade so I can read it afterward.



The trend-following and breakout strategies work really well when there's clear directional movement. But choppy, low-range markets? The agent just... sits there mostly. Which is actually correct behavior, but it's weird to watch when you're used to it being active.



A few things I've noticed:



\- The agent has gotten better at distinguishing between "nothing is happening" and "something is about to happen." Early on it would fire on fake breakouts a lot. Now it waits longer for confirmation and trades less overall in flat markets.



\- The written reasoning logs are actually more interesting during slow periods. It'll write things like "price is compressing, volume declining, no clear catalyst, standing aside" — which sounds obvious but seeing it articulate that rather than just staying flat is oddly reassuring.



\- We added a cooldown mechanism after a series of losses. If it takes more than X losing trades in a session, it stops for a bit. This was the single best change we made for sideways markets.



Curious what others here have done to handle this. Do you let your agent keep trading in low-vol environments or do you have some kind of market regime filter that slows it down?



The agent I'm using is [andmilo.com](http://andmilo.com) if anyone wants to see what the setup looks like — it's a non-custodial agent on Solana, still in alpha but the architecture is solid.

comments.author: sagesofthe4th_world
comments.body:
Thank you for sharing.

Yes, spent much time with AI trading. The AI itself has no idea tbh. You really just use it to automate rules you yourself have developed through back testing and figuring out the best strategies as you are doing.

But just throwing an AI at trading? It has no clue what it's doing or how to trade effectively. You could throw the latest AI models at some crypto data. Doesn't matter. If you don't give it the recipe, the strategy - it has no idea and just 'sounds good' when it's trading. .

replies.author: ok-hacker
replies.body:
Fair point and mostly agree. You do need to give it strategies to work with, you can't just plug in an LLM and expect alpha.



What milo does differently is it comes with pre-built strategies (meme sniper, momentum, etc.) that you configure via risk parameters, rather than expecting you to write your own from scratch. The AI layer is handling execution decisions within those guardrails, not figuring out what to trade on its own.



So it's less "AI magic" and more "strategy automation with an AI execution layer." Probably a more honest framing.

comments.author: ReportOwn3108
comments.body:
I use screenshots of charts instead and send it to claude where I condensed a word file full of skills, and events and much more

Every analysis it brings the file back up to apply the system on it

replies.author: ok-hacker
replies.body: That's a creative setup. The screenshot approach is interesting because it sidesteps the whole structured data pipeline -- you're basically letting the vision model do the parsing. Curious how it handles fast-moving charts where the visual snapshot might already be stale by the time Claude responds.

comments.author: No-Consequence-1779
comments.body: It keeps checking stuff. Trying to identify a top gainer early is pretty interesting.
